text: Hello, Catherine here from BBC Learning English.
zh: 大家好,我是BBC英语教学的凯瑟琳。

text: Just so you know, this programme is from the BBC Learning English archive and was first broadcast in September 2016 on our website.
zh: 提醒大家,本节目来自BBC英语教学的档案库,于2016年9月首次在我们的网站上播出。

text: And now, on with the show!
zh: 现在,让我们开始节目吧!

text: Hello and welcome to 6 Minute English.
zh: 你好,欢迎收听《六分钟英语》。

text: I'm Alice.
zh: 我是艾丽斯。

text: And I'm Neil.
zh: 我是尼尔。

text: Could you lend me some dosh, Neil?
zh: 尼尔,能借我点“铜钿”吗?

text: Sure.
zh: 当然可以。

text: How much do you need?
zh: 你需要多少?

text: A couple of smackers.
zh: 就几个“大子儿”。

text: You're sounding a bit strange today, Alice.
zh: 艾丽斯,你今天说话有点奇怪啊。

text: Yes, I know, Neil.
zh: 是的,我知道,尼尔。

text: Slang, or informal language used by a particular group, is the subject of today's show.
zh: 俚语,也就是某个特定群体使用的非正式语言,是今天节目的主题。

text: And I was just demonstrating a couple of slang words that mean money.
zh: 我刚才正好在示范几个表示“钱”的俚语词。

text: Dosh is a general term for money and a smacker is a British pound or US dollar.
zh: dosh是“钱”的通用说法,而smacker指的是一英镑或一美元。

text: OK, so Cockney rhyming slang is a type of slang.
zh: 好的,伦敦腔押韵俚语就是俚语的一种。

text: It's a coded language invented in the 19th century by Cockneys so they could speak in front of the police without being understood.
zh: 这是19世纪伦敦东区人发明的一种暗语,好让他们能在警察面前说话而不被听懂。

text: And still on the subject of money, I have a question for you, Alice.
zh: 还是说到钱这个话题,艾丽斯,我有个问题要问你。

text: Oh, OK.
zh: 哦,好的。

text: What's Cockney rhyming slang for money?
zh: 伦敦腔押韵俚语里“钱”怎么说?

text: Is it a bread, b honey or c dough?
zh: 是a. bread,b. honey,还是c. dough?

text: I think it's a bread.
zh: 我觉得是bread。

text: I bet you didn't know, Neil, that I'm a Cockney.
zh: 尼尔,我猜你不知道吧,我其实是个伦敦腔(Cockney)。

text: Oh, don't Adam and Eve it, Alice.
zh: 哦,艾丽斯,别“信不信由你”了吧(Adam and Eve)。

text: That's a pool pie!
zh: 那简直是“猪肉派”(pork pie)!

text: Adam and Eve means believe and pork pie means lie.
zh: Adam and Eve的意思是“相信”,pork pie的意思是“撒谎”。

text: Actually, you're right, I'm not a cockney.
zh: 其实你说得对,我不是伦敦腔。

text: To be considered a cockney, you need to be born within hearing distance of the bells of St Mary le Beau church in what is now the City of London.
zh: 要被认定为伦敦腔,你得出生在能听到圣玛丽勒波教堂钟声的范围内,也就是现在的伦敦城内。

text: Indeed.
zh: 确实如此。

text: Now, slang, as we've said, is colloquial or informal language and it's characteristic of of specific social groups.
zh: 我们说过,俚语是口语化或非正式的语言,是特定社会群体的特征。

text: We usually use it in informal conversation rather than in writing or more formal situations like a job interview.
zh: 我们通常在非正式的交谈中使用它,而不是在写作或求职面试这类更正式的场合。

text: We change the way we speak so that what we say is appropriate for a particular situation.
zh: 我们会根据场合调整说话方式,让所说的话符合当时的情境。

text: So you surprised me earlier, Alice, by talking about dosh and smackers because it didn't seem appropriate for presenting the show.
zh: 所以,尼尔,你之前听到我说dosh和smacker时才会觉得意外,因为这似乎不太适合用来主持节目。

text: Slang use is often frowned upon or disapproved of.
zh: 俚语的使用常常不被认可,甚至受到反对。

text: Let's listen to Jonathan Green, a lexicographer of slang, talking about who uses slang and how this has changed.
zh: 我们来听听俚语词典编纂者乔纳森·格林谈谈谁在使用俚语,以及这种情况是如何变化的。

text: Here he is on the Radio 4 programme Word of Mouth.
zh: 以下是他在Radio 4节目《口耳相传》(Word of Mouth)中的发言。

text: Slang does have a bad reputation, and I would say this comes from its earliest collection, which was of criminal slang in the 1500s, in the 16th century, and it was associated with bad people.
zh: 俚语确实名声不佳,我认为这源于它最早的记录,也就是16世纪1500年代的犯罪俚语,当时它和坏人联系在一起。

text: And inevitably that has lingered, but now, in the last 40, 50 years, it's changed.
zh: 这种印象不可避免地延续了下来,但在过去的四五十年里,情况发生了变化。

text: the definitions tend to stress different and jocular, funny, humorous, inventive, that kind of thing.
zh: 如今人们对它的定义更强调“不同寻常”、“诙谐有趣”、“幽默”、“富有创意”这些特点。

text: So, we have records of 16th century slang in collections or dictionaries,
zh: 所以,我们在16世纪的词典或汇编中能找到当时俚语的记录,

text: words used by criminals as a code so they could talk without being understood, and this bad reputation has lingered or been slow to disappear.
zh: 那是罪犯用来暗中交流、避免被外人听懂的暗语,这种不好的名声延续了很久,消失得很慢。

text: But for the last 50 years we've been using slang to be funny and creative, as well as to show belonging to a particular group.
zh: 但在过去的五十年里,我们使用俚语更多是为了幽默和创意,同时也用来表明自己属于某个群体。

text: And apparently we're very creative when talking about drinking and being drunk.
zh: 而且,显然我们在谈论喝酒和醉酒这件事上特别有创意。

text: The slang word booze, meaning alcohol, comes from the 13th century Dutch word boezen.
zh: 表示“酒”的俚语booze,来自13世纪荷兰语的boezen一词。

text: And there are hundreds of slang expressions to talk about drink and being drunk.
zh: 关于喝酒和醉酒,还有成百上千种俚语表达。

text: On the sauce, in your cups, half cut, hammered, squiffy, tipsy, wasted, legless and many, many more that are far too rude to mention in this programme.
zh: 比如on the sauce、in your cups、half cut、hammered、squiffy、tipsy、wasted、legless,还有很多很多,粗俗得没法在本节目里提。

text: Yes.
zh: 是啊。

text: So, while these terms might not be strictly acceptable or appropriate in formal contexts, they aren't offensive.
zh: 所以,虽然这些说法在正式场合可能不算得体,但它们并不算冒犯性的语言。

text: They are often amusing and help people bond in social groups.
zh: 它们往往带点幽默,能帮助人们在社交群体中建立联系。

text: By contrast, swear words or profanity means rude language that offends or upsets people.
zh: 相比之下,脏话或粗话指的是那种冒犯或惹恼别人的粗鲁语言。

text: And I'm not going to give any examples because that would be inappropriate and impolite, Alice.
zh: 艾丽斯,我可不会举例子,那样太不合适、太不礼貌了。

text: this.
zh: 这个。

text: OK.
zh: 好的。

text: Let's listen now to Jonathan Green and presenter Michael Rosen talking about jargon – another type of in -group language.
zh: 我们再来听听乔纳森·格林和主持人迈克尔·罗森谈论“行话”(jargon)——另一种群体内部使用的语言。

text: Jargon, what I would call is small -o occupational, small -p professional slang.
zh: 行话,我称之为“小写的职业性”“小写的专业性”俚语。

text: It's closed -off environment.
zh: 它是一种封闭环境中的语言。

text: You get legal jargon, you get naval jargon.
zh: 你会遇到法律行话,也会遇到航海行话。

text: I've been reading Patrick O 'Brien recently and that's a wash with futter plates and fiddying the deck.
zh: 我最近在读帕特里克·奥布莱恩的作品,里面满是futtock plate(艏材板)和fiddying the deck这类说法。

text: This is Radio 4, Jonathan.
zh: 乔纳森,这可是Radio 4,注意点。

text: Be careful.
zh: 小心点。

text: Jonathan Green in another segment of the BBC Radio 4 programme, Word of Mouth.
zh: 这是乔纳森·格林在BBC Radio 4节目《口耳相传》中另一段的发言。

text: So, he says jargon is occupational and professional, meaning people speak it at work, for example, lawyers and sailors.
zh: 他说,行话是职业性、专业性的,也就是说人们在工作中使用它,比如律师和水手。

text: A futtock plate is, I believe, an iron plate attached to the top of a ship's mast, but I don't know much about this subject.
zh: 我了解到,futtock plate是一种固定在船桅顶部的铁板,不过这方面我懂得不多。

text: That's the idea, though.
zh: 大概就是这个意思。

text: Jargon is the technical language belonging to a specific group.
zh: 行话就是属于某个特定群体的专业术语。

text: And to outsiders, this jargon is often hard to understand.
zh: 对外行人来说,这种行话往往很难理解。

text: Yes, and here in the studio I can use all the radio jargon that I like.
zh: 是的,在这个演播室里,我可以尽情使用各种广播行话。

text: Look at my faders here, Alice, going down and up and up and up.
zh: 艾丽斯,看看我这些推子(faders),推下去、拉上来,拉上来。

text: Just testing our levels!
zh: 就是在测试一下我们的音量!

text: Come on, leave the fader alone.
zh: 行了,别碰推子了。

text: It controls the level of sound on a studio deck.
zh: 它是用来控制录音室调音台上声音音量的。

text: Now, it's time for the answer to today's quiz question, Neil.
zh: 现在,是时候公布今天的问答题答案了,尼尔。

text: I asked you, what's Cockney rhyming slang for money?
zh: 我问你,伦敦腔押韵俚语里“钱”怎么说?

text: Is it a bread, b honey or c dough?
zh: 是a. bread,b. honey,还是c. dough?

text: And I said a bread.
zh: 我说是bread。

text: And you were right, Alice.
zh: 你说对了,艾丽斯。

text: Cockney rhyming slang uses just the first word of a phrase that rhymes with the word we're trying to disguise.
zh: 伦敦腔押韵俚语只取一个与要隐藏的词押韵的短语中的第一个词。

text: So money becomes bread and honey, but we just say bread.
zh: 所以“钱”变成了bread and honey,但我们只说bread。

text: OK, so let's recap on the words we've learnt today.
zh: 好了,我们来复习一下今天学到的词。

text: They are slang, dosh, smacker,
zh: 它们是:slang(俚语)、dosh(钱)、smacker(一元/一镑钱)、

text: cockney rhyming slang, colloquial, frowned upon, lingered, booze, swear, profanity, jargon.
zh: cockney rhyming slang(伦敦腔押韵俚语)、colloquial(口语化的)、frowned upon(不被认可的)、lingered(挥之不去)、booze(酒)、swear(说脏话)、profanity(粗话)、jargon(行话)。

text: Well, that's the end of today's 6 Minute English.
zh: 好了,今天的《六分钟英语》就到这里。

text: Please join us again soon.
zh: 欢迎大家下次继续收听。

text: Bye bye.
zh: 再见。

text: Goodbye.
zh: 再见。
